name: Elizabeth Mine
short_description: Historic mica mine in central Vermont offering collectors access to pegmatite specimens and feldspar crystals. Moderate hiking required on scenic woodland terrain.
description:
Elizabeth Mine is a historic mineral collecting site near Strafford, Vermont, known for its pegmatitic deposits. The mine sits at moderate elevation in Orange County's metamorphic terrain, where collectors can find mica sheets, feldspar crystals, and quartz specimens. The site offers a moderately challenging experience for rockhounders interested in New England's mineral diversity.

The geological setting reflects Vermont's position in the New England Uplands, where Paleozoic metamorphic rocks were intruded by granitic pegmatites. These pegmatitic formations created ideal conditions for large crystal growth, particularly mica and feldspar. The Elizabeth Mine area showcases classic pegmatite mineralogy characteristic of Vermont's White Mountain Magma Series.

The collecting area features mixed forest terrain with moderate slope, requiring sturdy footwear and a willingness to navigate uneven ground. The mine workings themselves may be partially overgrown, adding to the adventure. Collectors should bring proper tools for specimen extraction and be prepared for the natural hazards of old mining areas.

This location appeals to intermediate collectors seeking high-quality mica and pegmatite specimens without traveling to distant sites. The historical significance of Vermont's mica mining heritage adds educational value to visits here.
history: Elizabeth Mine operated as a mica mine during Vermont's mining boom in the 19th and early 20th centuries, when mica was in high demand for electrical insulation and industrial applications. The site sits within Vermont's metamorphic belt, where Paleozoic schists and granites intruded by pegmatitic bodies created ideal conditions for mica and feldspar crystal formation over 300 million years ago.
tips: Search along the mine walls and in weathered talus piles where frost action has naturally freed specimens. Mica sheets often peel away from exposed faces on the hillside - tap gently with a chisel to encourage clean removal. Look for pegmatite outcrops showing the characteristic large crystals and coarse grain structure. Bring a small hammer, cold chisel, and gently widen existing fractures rather than striking fresh rock.
terrain_notes: Elevation 1316 feet with moderate slope covered in mixed deciduous and conifer forest. Uneven rocky terrain with exposed schist and pegmatite outcrops. Walking distance from parking approximately 0.5-1 mile through woodland. Footing is challenging due to roots, loose rock, and uneven ground - sturdy hiking boots required.
best_season: May - September. Mountain terrain in central Vermont is most accessible during warmer months when trails dry out and weather is stable. Spring mud and winter snow create hazardous conditions on the moderate slopes.
written_directions: From Strafford, take VT Route 132 east toward Topsham approximately 2 miles. Look for rough forest service roads or unmarked pullouts on the north side. Park safely off the road and hike upslope through mixed woodland for 0.5-1 mile to reach the old mine workings. GPS coordinates are 43.821207, -72.337437. Note that access may require crossing private property - always seek permission.
road_conditions: Forest service road or unmarked pullout. High clearance vehicle recommended for rough forest roads. Park well away from active logging operations.
parking_notes: Limited informal parking at forest service pullouts. No developed parking area. Park responsibly off road to avoid blocking access.
collection_rules: Private land - obtain permission from property owner before collecting. Respect posted boundaries and posted signs. No mechanized excavation. Take only what you can carry. Leave the site in better condition than you found it.
rules:
Obtain permission from landowner before collecting
No commercial collection
Respect property boundaries
No motorized equipment
Pack out what you pack in
nearest_services: Strafford village has limited services approximately 2 miles south. Sharon, Vermont (5 miles south) offers gas and food. Hanover, New Hampshire has full services approximately 20 miles south.
accessibility_notes: Moderate hiking over uneven, rocky woodland terrain with significant elevation gain. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots essential. Trail unmarked and may be overgrown. Not suitable for those with limited mobility or young children. Footing hazards include exposed roots, loose rock, and pegmatite outcrops.

faq.question: What minerals can I find rockhounding at Elizabeth Mine near Strafford, Vermont?
faq.answer: Elizabeth Mine is best known for mica sheets, feldspar crystals, and quartz specimens. These minerals occur in the pegmatite deposits that form the mine. Tourmaline is also occasionally found. The site represents classic New England pegmatite mineralogy from Vermont's metamorphic belt.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Elizabeth Mine in Vermont free or do I need to pay a fee?
faq.answer: Elizabeth Mine is on private land, so there is no fee. However, you must obtain permission from the landowner before collecting. Always check locally in Strafford for current access conditions and contact information for property owners.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Elizabeth Mine?
faq.answer: May through September is ideal. Vermont's mountain terrain at 1316 feet elevation experiences heavy mud in spring and snow in winter. Summer and early fall offer the most stable weather and dry footing for hiking the moderate slope to the mine site.

faq.question: Do I need any special permits or equipment to collect at Elizabeth Mine, Vermont?
faq.answer: No permit is required, but landowner permission is essential. Bring a small hammer and cold chisel to tap mica sheets free from outcrops, sturdy hiking boots for uneven terrain, and a pack to carry specimens. Forest service roads may require high clearance.

faq.question: Is Elizabeth Mine rockhounding site beginner-friendly or suitable for families?
faq.answer: Elizabeth Mine is better suited for intermediate collectors with hiking experience. The moderate terrain, abandoned mine hazards, and need for landowner permission make it less ideal for beginners or young children. Age 12+ recommended with proper supervision and equipment.

faq.question: How do I get to Elizabeth Mine from Strafford for rockhounding?
faq.answer: From Strafford village, take VT Route 132 east approximately 2 miles. Look for rough forest service roads or unmarked pullouts on the north side, then hike 0.5-1 mile upslope through mixed woodland to reach the old mine workings at GPS 43.821207, -72.337437. Confirm access with locals first.

meta_description: Find mica, feldspar, and quartz at historic Elizabeth Mine near Strafford, Vermont. Private land collecting with moderate hiking. Pegmatite specimens.
